coredata.dc:title: Core-shell AuNRs@Ag-enhanced and magnetic separation-assisted SERS immunosensing platform for amantadine detection in animal-derived foods
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jingying
Pan, Mingfei
Liu, Kaixin
Xie, Xiaoqian
Wang, Shan
Hong, Liping
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: A core-shell AuNRs@Ag-enhanced and magnetic separation-assisted surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) immunosensing platform was developed for rapid, accurate and sensitive detection of amantadine (AMD) contaminant in animal-derived foods. This SERS immunosensing platform works in a competitive strategy, which used the 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA) labeled AuNRs@Ag with AMD-BSA immobilized as a signal probe and magnetic beads (MBs) with mAbs covalently attached to the surface as a capture probe. It was found that the synthesized core-shell AuNRs@Ag could significantly enhance the SERS signal of the reporter 4-MBA (EF: 2.91 × 107), and the coating of Ag shell effectively avoided the shedding of 4-MBA, thereby improving the sensitivity and stability of SERS analysis. The use of MBs not only overcomes the shortcoming of mAbs inactivation, but also simplifies the detection procedure, improving the detection efficiency. This SERS immunosensing platform was demonstrated to have a linear response in a wide AMD concentration range of 0.01–50 μg/L with high sensitivity (limit of detection (LOD): 0.0038 μg/L) and specificity. For actual animal-derived foods (chicken, milk, egg), acceptable recoveries of AMD (82.0–106.0%) at three spiked levels (0.5, 5, 50 μg/kg) were obtained with relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 5) ranging from 4.7% to 9.6%. The whole detection procedure can be completed within 30 min. These results demonstrated that the proposed SERS immunosensing platform was reliable and sensitive in the quantitative analysis of AMD and could be applied as an effective tool for rapid screening for AMD-contaminated foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SERS immunosensing platform
Amantadine
Core-shell AuNRs@Ag
Magnetic separation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of AMD using SERS immunosensing platform
Principle of SERS immunosensing platform for AMD detection
Analytical performance of SERS immunosensing platform
Analysis of AMD in real food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2017YFC1600402 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31972147 ), Tianjin Graduate Research Innovation Project (No. 2020YJSS127 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of the Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (No. 2021MS03077 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV spectra (A) and TEM images (B) of AuNRs and AuNRs@Ag synthesized using AgNO3 solution from 50 to 250 μL. (C) UV spectra of AuNRs4-MBA@Ag and AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-AMD-BSA.
Characterizations of capture probe. TEM image (A) and XRD pattern (B) of MBs. Zeta potential measurements (C) and magnetic hysteresis curves (D) of the MBs and MBs-mAbs.

(A) SERS spectra of AuNRs4-MBA using different volume (5–150 μL) of 4-MBA (0.1 mM). (B) Plots of the Raman intensity of 4-MBA at 1585 cm−1 corresponding to A. (C) SERS intensity of Au4-MBA@AgNRs using different volumes (0–250 μL) of AgNO3 (10 mM) added in AuNRs4MBA (1 mL); (D) Plots of the Raman intensity of 4-MBA at 1585 cm−1 corresponding to C. Error bars show the standard deviation of three repeated experiments.
Optimization of the amount of anti-AMD mAbs for MBs modification.

(A) SERS spectra acquired with various AMD concentrations. (B) The relationship between SERS intensity at 1585 cm−1 and AMD concentrations. (C) Series of SERS spectra of 1.0 μg/L AMD collected from 10 batches. (D) Color-coded intensity 2D SERS mapping at 1585 cm−1 with the AMD concentration of 1.0 μg/L. (E) SERS intensity at 1585 cm−1 for AMD detection by the SERS-based immunoassay for different storage times (0–30 days). (F) The CR values of SERS immunosensing platform for AMD, rimantadine, adamantane, ribavirin, enrofloxacin and chloramphenicol. Each of the points is the average of three detection data and shown with a standard error bar.
Schematic illustration of SERS immunosensing platform for AMD detection.
Results of AMD detection in animal-derived foods using the developed SERS immunosensing platform.
An overview and comparison of the recent strategies for AMD detection.

head.ce:title: Core-shell AuNRs@Ag-enhanced and magnetic separation-assisted SERS immunosensing platform for amantadine detection in animal-derived foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education of China, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education of China, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education of China, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A core-shell AuNRs@Ag-enhanced and magnetic separation-assisted surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) immunosensing platform was developed for rapid, accurate and sensitive detection of amantadine (AMD) contaminant in animal-derived foods. This SERS immunosensing platform works in a competitive strategy, which used the 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA) labeled AuNRs@Ag with AMD-BSA immobilized as a signal probe and magnetic beads (MBs) with mAbs covalently attached to the surface as a capture probe. It was found that the synthesized core-shell AuNRs@Ag could significantly enhance the SERS signal of the reporter 4-MBA (EF: 2.91 × 107), and the coating of Ag shell effectively avoided the shedding of 4-MBA, thereby improving the sensitivity and stability of SERS analysis. The use of MBs not only overcomes the shortcoming of mAbs inactivation, but also simplifies the detection procedure, improving the detection efficiency. This SERS immunosensing platform was demonstrated to have a linear response in a wide AMD concentration range of 0.01–50 μg/L with high sensitivity (limit of detection (LOD): 0.0038 μg/L) and specificity. For actual animal-derived foods (chicken, milk, egg), acceptable recoveries of AMD (82.0–106.0%) at three spiked levels (0.5, 5, 50 μg/kg) were obtained with relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 5) ranging from 4.7% to 9.6%. The whole detection procedure can be completed within 30 min. These results demonstrated that the proposed SERS immunosensing platform was reliable and sensitive in the quantitative analysis of AMD and could be applied as an effective tool for rapid screening for AMD-contaminated foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Amantadine (AMD) is a broad-spectrum antiviral drug that can effectively prevent and treat viral infections by interfering with M2 proton channel of influenza A virus [1,2]. Because of its excellent antiviral ability, AMD has been widely used for the prevention and treatment of swine infectious gastroenteritis and avian influenza [3]. Researchers have verified that long-term abuse of AMD can increase the resistance of influenza strains, which was harmful to human health [4,5]. Therefore, relevant laws and regulations prohibiting the use of AMD on animals have been announced and implemented in many countries [6]. Due to the strong antiviral ability and low-cost, AMD is still illegally used to treat diseases in the process of livestock and poultry breeding, posing a huge threat to people's health [7]. Therefore, it is very necessary to detect and monitor the content of AMD contaminant in animal-derived foods.

At present, the methods based on the chromatographic instruments are still the main strategies for AMD contaminant detection. Among them, the liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) method is widely recognized for its high accuracy and sensitivity [8,9]. Due to the limitation of large-scale equipment, a complicated and tedious pretreatment process for animal-derived foods is required. Meanwhile the requirements of expensive equipment and skilled operators also restrict the popularization and application of such methods in on-site and rapid screening of large number of samples. To address these challenges, other strategies including enzyme-linked immunosorbent/immunochromatographic assay [10,11] and nanomaterials-based fluorescent/electrochemical sensors [12–14] have been developed successively for AMD screening. These methods have significant advantages in high-throughput and on-site testing, but the fragility of enzymes and the low selectivity of nanomaterials to analyte may reduce signal stability or even lead to false signals, resulting in low accuracy. Therefore, a convenient, sensitive, and accurate analysis strategy for AMD contaminant in animal-derived foods is still urgently needed.

Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) has become one of the most promising analysis strategies due to its unique advantages such as high sensitivity, unique spectral fingerprints, non-invasive data collection and fast response speed [15,16]. Although SERS can perform spectral fingerprint analysis on targeted molecules without labeling, the components in food matrix may produce interfering Raman peaks, which affect the detection accuracy. At present, the low specificity of SERS and the complexity of food matrix are the key issues that limit its further application in the field of food contaminant detection [17,18]. SERS immunosensing strategies combined the high sensitivity of SERS analysis with the specific recognition of immune reaction, which can meet the requirements of modern food detection, and is a very promising tools for food contaminants detection [19,20]. The construction of SERS signal probe is crucial to a high-performance SERS immunosensing platform. Metal nanoparticles (NPs) with controllable shape, such as nanospheres, nanorods (NRs), nanoflowers, nanostars are excellent candidates for the fabrication of SERS signal probe due to their intrinsically intense plasmonic response as well as the potential formation of “hotspots” [21,22]. It has been theoretically predicted and experimentally confirmed that due to the anisotropic distribution of the electromagnetic field near the surface of non-spherical particles, the NRs exhibit higher SERS enhancement compared with spherical NPs [23]. Additionally, due to the coupling effect between the different metals, the bimetallic core-shell NPs have more advantages in physical and chemical properties than the single metal, and can be used to prepare highly efficient SERS probes [24,25]. For core-shell NPs, the gap of core-shell can produce intrametallic “hot spot”, which dramatically improved the Raman enhancement effect. Simultaneously, organic molecules used to provide characteristic signals can be embedded in the core-shell gap, effectively avoiding falling off the surface of NPs and preventing the interference from the surrounding environment, which can improve the detection stability. Therefore, due to the larger surface ectromagnetic field of the Ag shell and the plasma coupling effect between AuNRs core and Ag shell, AuNRs@Ag is considered to have significant SERS activity and shows great application prospect in SERS analysis.

Herein, we proposed an indirect SERS immunosensing platform for rapid, accurate and sensitive detection of AMD in animal-derived foods. This SERS platform applied the AMD-BSA conjugate immobilized 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA)-labeled AuNRs@Ag as SERS signal probe and monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) covalently attached MBs as capture probe. The AuNRs@Ag with core-shell structure were used as SERS substrate to amplify Raman signal. The 4-MBA with an intense characteristic peak at 1585 cm−1 was selected as the Raman reporter molecule to achieve reliable quantitative analysis of target AMD in complex media. And the MBs used can provide a solid carrier for immune reaction, simplify the detection procedure and shorten the detection time. This present AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-based SERS immunosensing platform exhibited an extremely high sensitivity towards AMD with limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.0038 μg/L. The whole Raman detection procedure can be completed within 30 min. These results demonstrated that the developed AuNRs4-MBA@Ag based SERS immunosensing platform offered an effective strategy for the analysis of AMD contaminant in food matrix.

The target analyte AMD (99.9%) and other standards adamantane, rimantadine, ribavirin, enrofloxacin and chloramphenicol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.9%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%) and 4-MBA (99.0%) were also purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethyl-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hy-droxysuccinimide (NHS), diethylene Glycol (DEG), polyacrylic acid (PAA, MW: ~ 45,000) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) with a purity of at least 99%. Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99%), 2-(N-morpholine) ethanesulfonic acid (MES, 98.0%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The mAbs against AMD (5 mg/mL) and AMD-bovine serum albumin conjugate (AMD-BSA, 3.5 mg/mL) were purchased from Shandong Lvdu Biology Co. Lt. (Shandong, China). Other solvents used in this work were at analytical grade and supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q device (18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore, Molsheim, France) and used throughout the experiment.

UV absorption spectrum of colloidal AuNRs@Ag was recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 300, Thermo, Japan). TEM images were acquired from Transmission Electron Microscope (talos F200X G2, Thermo, USA) operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was performed on an x-ray diffractometer (D8 advance, Bruker, Germany) diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1542 nm) using a current of 40 mA and a voltage of 40 kV. Zeta potential data was acquired by a particle size and potential analyzer (Nicomp380Z3000, PSS, USA). The hysteresis loop was measured on a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (HH-15, AOD, China) with a magnetic field strength of 1.0 T. The Raman spectra were measured using a microscopic Raman spectrometer (inVia Reflex, Renishaw, England) equipped with a 50 × objective confocal microscope and 785 nm laser. All measurements were gathered from arbitrarily chosen spots with 10 s integration time and 5% laser power.

The SERS signal probe is composed of AuNRs core, Raman reporter molecule 4-MBA, Ag shell and AMD-BSA conjugate ( Scheme 1 A). First, the AuNRs were synthesized following a kinetically controlled seeded growth strategy according to the previous report with slight modification [26]. Briefly, 5.0 mL of HAuCl4 solution (0.5 mM) was mixed with 5.0 mL of CTAB (0.2 M) through stirring at 2000 rpm. Then, 0.6 mL of ice-cold NaBH4 (10 mM) was added into the mixture and stirred at 30 °C for 2 h to obtain the Au seed solution. In a round-bottom flask, 40.0 mL of HAuCl4 solution (1 mM) and 40.0 mL of CTAB solution (0.2 M) were mixed, then 0.8 mL of AgNO3 (10 mM), 0.8 mL of HCl (2.0 M) and 0.64 mL of L -ascorbic acid (0.1 M) were added successively to prepare the growth solution. Finally, 0.28 mL of the pre-prepared Au seed solution was added and reacted overnight at 30 °C. The resulting solution was centrifuged twice at 11,000 rpm for 10 min and the AuNRs precipitate was dispersed in ultrapure water (40.0 mL). The Raman reporter molecule 4-MBA were modified on the surface of AuNRs by the AuS bond. 100 μL of 4-MBA (0.1 mM) was incubated with AuNRs solution (1.0 mL) for 5 h to prepare SERS active probe AuNRs4-MBA. Centrifugation was performed to remove the unbound 4-MBA molecules and re-dispersed in 1.0 mL of ultrapure water for further use.

To obtain the Raman active substrate with stronger enhancement effect, AuNRs4-MBA@Ag with core-shell structure was synthesized by reducing AgNO3 in the solution of AuNRs4-MBA. 1.0 mL of AuNRs4-MBA solution was mixed with 5.0 mL of CTAB (0.04 M), then 130 μL of L -ascorbic acid (0.1 M), 150 μL of AgNO3 (0.01 M), and 290 μL of NaOH (0.1 M) was added successively to initiate the reduction reaction of Ag+. After being stirred for 0.5 h at 30 °C, the AuNRs4-MBA@Ag product was separated by centrifugation and re-dispersed in ultrapure water (6.0 mL). 1.0 mL of AuNRs4-MBA@Ag was mixed with 15 μL of the AMD-BSA solution (3.5 mg/mL) and stirred gently at room temperature for 2 h. Finally, the SERS signal probe (AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-AMD-BSA) was separated centrifugally and re-suspended using ultrapure water (1.0 mL). Prior to use, the prepared SERS probe was stored at 4 °C to maintain its stability and biological activity.

The capture probe was prepared by coupling the mAbs against AMD onto the surface of carboxylated MBs by covalent binding ( Scheme 1 B). MBs were prepared by high-temperature hydrolysis method according to the literature [27]. Briefly, 20.0 mL of NaOH-DEG solution (2.5 M) was heated at 120 °C for 1 h and cooled to 70 °C to prepare the stock solution. 15.0 mL of DEG solution dissolving PAA (0.24 M) and FeCl3 (0.24 M) was heated to 220 °C for 30 min under intense agitation to form a transparent light-yellow solution. After that, 1.85 mL of NaOH-DEG stock solution was injected and kept the temperature at 210 °C for 1 h. The produced carboxylated MBs were separated by magnetic separation and washed at least three times using ultrapure water. All steps of the synthesis process were carried out under the protection of N2.

For coupling the mAbs onto the carboxylated MBs surface, 10 mg of the prepared MBs were washed thoroughly and dispersed in 1.0 mL of MES buffer solution (pH = 4). Then, 200 μL of the mixture of EDC (0.4 M) and NHS (0.1 M) were added and incubated for 30 min at 30 °C to activate the carboxyl groups of MBS. After washed thoroughly using phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) and re-suspended in PBS (1.0 mL), the activated MBs was mixed with 20 μL of mAbs (5 mg/mL) and the mixture was stirred continuously for 2 h. Furthermore, 1.0 mL of BSA (2%) was added and incubated for 1 h to block the excess exposure sites on MBs surface. Finally, the MBs-mAbs products (SERS capture probe) were washed using 0.01 M PBS containing 0.05% Tween-20 and suspended in 1.0 mL PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AMD using SERS immunosensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The developed SERS immunosensing platform works based on a standard protocol of competitive immunoassay, which applied the SERS signal probe (AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-AMD-BSA) and capture probe (MBs-mAbs). Detailed procedure was briefly as follows: 20 μL of AMD standard working solution at various concentrations (0.001–100 μg/L) was individually mixed with 100 μL of AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-AMD-BSA signal probe solution. Then 20 μL of MBs-mAbs capture probe was added and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Magnetic separation was applied to obtain the supernatants containing unbound signal probes which was used for Raman analysis. The resulting Raman spectra was recorded and the Raman intensity at 1585 cm−1 (characteristic peak of 4-MBA) was applied to evaluate the AMD content in samples.

Pure milk, egg, and chicken muscle purchased from a local supermarket (Tianjin, China) were applied to evaluate the feasibility of the developed SERS immunosensing platform. The sample was accurately weighed (1.0 g) and spiked AMD at three levels (0.5, 5 and 50 μg/kg). After standing for 2 h, 5.0 mL of the extracting agent (methanol: 1% acetic acid = 1:10, v/v) was then added. The mixture was homogenized for 5 min using a vortex machine and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred into a clean centrifuge tube and analyzed using the developed SERS immunosensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of SERS immunosensing platform for AMD detection
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 has illustrated the fabrication and working principle of the developed SERS immunosensing platform using AMD-BSA immobilized on the 4-MBA labeled AuNRs@Ag as SERS signal probe, mAbs covalently attached to MBs surface as capture probe. The detection for AMD was achieved based on a competitive strategy. The signal probe (AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-AMD-BSA) can compete with the target analyte AMD to bind to the capture probe (MBs-mAbs), resulting in a positive correlation between the intensity of the free signal probe and the AMD concentration after magnetic separation. According to the SERS intensity of the free signal probe at 1585 cm−1 (4-MBA), the quantitative analysis of AMD in the sample is enabled.

This study used core-shell AuNRs@Ag as SERS active substrate to generate "hot spots" and 4-MBA embedded in the AuNRs@Ag gap as Raman reporter to produce strong and stable Raman signal. Ag shell with an appropriate thickness could not only greatly enhance and stabilize the Raman signal of 4-MBA, but also offer the sites for the AMD-BSA conjunction.

Core-shell AuNRs@Ag was prepared through depositing a certain thickness of Ag shell on the AuNRs core synthesized by a seed-mediated method. Fig. 1 A has shown that the UV spectra of synthesized AuNRs and AuNRs@Ag using different amount of AgNO3 solution (10 mM). It was clearly observed that the AuNRs had longitudinal and transverse plasma resonance absorption bands at 850 and 520 nm, respectively. With the addition of AgNO3, the longitudinal and transverse plasma bands occurred obvious blue shifts, and a new characteristic absorption band appeared around 400 nm, which is contribution to the formation of Ag shell on the surface of AuNRs. With the increase of AgNO3, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of the Ag shell with a certain thickness dominates, resulting in the transverse plasma bands of AuNRs quickly decayed and disappeared and the SPR band around 400 nm gradually enhanced.

TEM was applied to characterize the morphology of the synthesized AuNRs and core-shell AuNRs@Ag. As shown in Fig. 1 B, the aspect ratio of the synthesized AuNRs was 4.0. After adding AgNO3, Ag shell was gradually formed on the AuNRs surface through the reduction of Ag+. Owing to the difference in atomic mass between Au and Ag, an obvious core-shell heterostructure can be observed. The thickness of Ag shell on the AuNRs surface had a significant positive correlation with the amount of AgNO3 solution added. Simultaneously, the deposited Ag shell could cause the AuNRs core to be etched, decreasing the aspect ratio of the core-shell material. When 150 μL of AgNO3 solution (10 mM) was added, the thickness of Ag shell on AuNRs surface reached 6 nm. Further increase the volume of AgNO3 (250 μL), AuNRs@Ag with significantly irregular morphology were formed and the thickness of the Ag shell could reach 10 nm. Therefore, the morphology of AuNRs@Ag and the thickness of the outer Ag shell can be effectively regulated by adjusting the amount of AgNO3.

The AMD-BSA conjugate was coupled to the prepared AuNRs4-MBA@Ag to fabricate the SERS signal probe. Fig. 1 C has illustrated the UV spectra of AuNRs4-MBA@Ag and AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-AMD-BSA probe. It was observed that the characteristic absorption peak of BSA (280 nm) appeared on the AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-AMD-BSA spectra, indicating that the SERS signal probes were successfully prepared.

Fig. 2 A, B has shown the TEM and XRD results of MBs prepared by high-temperature hydrolysis method. The MBs was demonstrated to have a relatively uniform morphology with an average diameter of 300 ± 25 nm. Typical XRD peaks occurred at 2θ = 30.4°, 35.7°, 37.3°, 43.2°, 53.7° and 62.8°, corresponding to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of Fe3O4 (JCPDS no. 79-0629), respectively, which confirmed the good crystalline structure of the prepared MBs. Furthermore, the mAbs were covalently coupled on the carboxylated MBs surface to prepare capture probe (MBs-mAbs). From the results of Zeta potential (Fig. 2 C), the synthesized MBs has a primordial potential at − 12.4 mV. After coupling with the negatively charged mAbs, the Zeta potential tends to be more negative (−20.8 mV), revealing that the mAbs were effectively coupled on carboxylated MBs surface. The magnetic hysteresis loop was recorded to evaluate the magnetic property of MBs and MBs-mAbs (Fig. 2 D). The MBs exhibited a super-paramagnetic property with a saturation magnetization value of 105.4 emu g−1. The magnetization value of MBs-mAbs showed a slightly decreased (84.4 emu g−1) after covalently coupling the mAbs onto the surface, illustrating that the capture probe still retained a favorable magnetic property. Meanwhile, from the inset photo in Fig. 2 D, the collection of capture probe can be finished within 10 s, indicating that the use of MBs can simplify the separation process and shorten the detection time. All these results demonstrated that capable MBs-mAbs were prepared and could be applied as capture probes of the developed SERS immunosensing platform.

The amount of 4-MBA, AgNO3 and mAbs were crucial parameters affecting the performance of SERS immunosensing platform, which were optimized in detail. Core-shell bimetallic AuNRs@Ag was applied as Raman active substrate and the reporter 4-MBA was inserted into the core-shell gap to provide characteristic Raman signals. The effective load of 4-MBA has an important influence on detection sensitivity and stability. Varying volumes (5, 25, 50, 100, 150 μL) of 4-MBA solution (0.1 mM) were used to react with 1.0 mL of AuNRs, and the AuNRs4-MBA product was collected by centrifugation and resuspended for SERS analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, the Raman signal of the 4-MBA at 1585 cm−1 (characteristic peak) exhibited an obvious enhancement with the increase of 4-MBA dosage and reached the maximum at a volume of 100 μL (Fig. 3 B). Adding more volume (150 μL) of 4-MBA caused a slight decrease on Raman signal intensity at 1585 cm−1. Excessive addition of 4-MBA can also cause the aggregation of AuNRs, affecting the detection stability. Therefore, 100 μL of 4-MBA amount (0.1 mM) was selected for the fabrication of SERS signal probe.

The compact shell can effectively prevent the Raman reporter from detaching from AuNRs to the solution and prevent the interference from the surroundings, which can significantly improve the stability and sensitivity of the signal probe. By adjusting the amount of AgNO3 (10 mM) added, Ag shells of different thickness were formed on the AuNRs4-MBA core, ensuring the effective load of 4-MBA and improving the Raman enhancement effect. Fig. 3 C has shown the Raman spectra of AuNRs4-MBA@Ag with Ag shell of different thicknesses. It was found that the Raman signal intensity had obvious changes as the volume of AgNO3 increased. Due to the larger surface ectromagnetic field of the Ag shell as well as the enhanced plasmonic coupling effect between the AuNRs core and Ag shell, the Raman intensity gradually increases with the increase of the Ag shell thickness (0–200 μL). However, due to that the Raman probe (4-MBA) is embedded in the core-shell gap, the excessive thickness of Ag shell may affect the output of the signal, causing Raman signal intensity decrease (200–250 μL). As shown in Fig. 3 C and D, the signal intensity at 1585 cm−1 (4-MBA) reached to the highest when 200 μL of AgNO3 (10 mM) was used (Ag shell thickness: 8 nm), which was chosen for further application.

The SERS enhancement factor (EF) of the prepared AuNRs@Ag were calculated by the following equation (Detailed analysis was listed in Supporting information):
EF =
I
SERS
I
Raman
×
N
Raman
N
SERS
where I SERS is the SERS intensity of 4-MBA, I Raman is the Raman intensity of non-SERS detection, N SERS is the number of 4-MBA molecules probed in SERS, and N Raman is the number of 4-MBA molecule probed in Raman measurement. The EF of the Au@AgNRs with an 8 nm Ag shell for 4-MBA was calculated to be about 2.91 × 107, showing an extremely high SERS activity.

The amount of anti-AMD mAbs on the surface of carboxy-functionalized MBs affects the immunecoupling efficiency, which may affect the detection sensitivity. Different amount of mAbs (1, 5, 10, 20, 40 and 80 μL, 5 mg/mL) were tested for coupling on the MBs surface. The inhibition ratio (S 10/S 0) was calculated and compared, in which S 0, S 10 means the intensity of Raman characteristic signal at 1585 cm−1 at AMD concentration of 0 and 10 μg/L, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4, with the increase of mAbs amount (0–20 μL), the S 10/S 0 gradually increased, indicating that more signal probes combined with the capture probe, which improved the sensitivity of immunoassay. However, more mAbs used for MBs modification may lead to excessive mAbs on its surface, which would produce steric hindrance, resulting in the interference binding with the target or antibody shedding in the detection process. The S 10/S 0 value at more mAbs solution (> 20 μL) decreased, signifying the decrease in sensitivity. Therefore, 20 μL of mAbs, which has the highest S 10/S 0 value, was selected for the modification of MBs and further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of SERS immunosensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5A showed the obtained SERS spectra of the supernatant containing the free signal probes by adding varying concentrations of AMD (0, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 25.0, 50.0, 100.0 μg/L). It was found that with the increase of AMD concentration, the SERS intensity of 4-MBA at 1585 cm−1 increased significantly. Noticeable spectral features of 4-MBA can be easily observed even when the concentration of AMD was as low as 0.001 μg/L. Fig. 5 B illustrated the corresponding relationship between the SERS signal intensity obtained at 1585 cm−1 and AMD concentrations, which represents a relatively wide response range (0.01–50.0 μg/L) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9900 and a lower LOD (S/N = 3) for AMD was calculated to be 0.0038 μg/L.

The reproducibility of the developed SERS immunosensing platform was evaluated by comparing the SERS spectra acquired from different batches. At the AMD concentration of 1.0 μg/L, the SERS signal intensity at 1585 cm−1 from 10 different batches has an average value of 8453 (a.u.) with a lower RSD value of 6.2% (Fig. 5 C). To assess the uniformity of SERS signal distribution, a point-to-point Raman mapping test at AMD concentration of 1.0 μg/L was carried out on a selected area (100 × 100 µm2). The obtained Raman intensity distribution at 1585 cm−1 in this area was shown in Fig. 5 D. The RSD calculated from the intensity at 1585 cm−1 was 4.7%, indicating that the Raman signal distribution is relatively uniform. In the time-dependent experiments, the signal probe (AuNRs4-MBA@Ag-AMD-BSA) and capture probe (MBs-mAbs) were stored at 4 °C for different time and used for AMD testing (1.0 μg/L). As illustrated in Fig. 5 E, as the storage time increased, the intensity of characteristic Raman peak (1585 cm−1) decreased slightly, but it still maintained nearly 86% of the original value after 30 days. These results verified that the developed SERS immunosensing platform had good reproducibility and stability in the quantitative analysis of AMD.

In practical applications, other substances in food can interfere with the identification of the target, especially when the target concentration is low. Therefore, it is necessary to evaluate and verify the specificity of the constructed indirect SERS immunosensing platform. Chemicals with similar structure to AMD including rimantadine, adamantane and three broad-spectrum antibiotics enrofloxacin, ribavirin and chloramphenicol were selected as analyte to evaluate the specificity of the proposed SERS-based immunoassay. A series of standard working solutions of the above compounds (0.1, 1.0, 10.0 and 100.0 μg/L) were analyzed to obtain the SERS signal intensity at 1585 cm−1 to calculate the value of cross-reactivity (CR). This proposed SERS immunosensing platform was demonstrated to have slight cross-reactivity with AMD analogs (CR: 28.3% (rimantadine), 21.8% (adamantane)) and no cross reaction with ribavirin, enrofloxacin, chloramphenicol (CR < 0.01%), indicating the great specificity of the SERS immunosensing platform for AMD analysis (Fig. 5 F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AMD in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the capabilities of the developed SERS immunosensing platform in practical applications, three AMD levels (0.5, 5, 50 μg/kg) were spiked to real animal-derived foods including chicken muscle, eggs, and milk. After simple sample pretreatment, AMD was quantitatively analyzed by evaluating the intensity of Raman signal at 1585 cm−1. As shown in Table 1, acceptable recoveries ranging from 82.0% to 106.0% were obtained in all tested samples with RSDs from 4.7% to 9.6% (n = 5), indicating that the proposed SERS-based immunoassay had good accuracy and reliability. The constructed SERS immunosensing platform can complete the whole detection procedure within 30 min. Compared with other reported strategies for AMD detection ( Table 2), the proposed SERS immunosensing strategy showed the merits of rapid, accurate and sensitive in the quantitative analysis of AMD, and could be applied for rapid screening of AMD-contaminated foods.

This study proposed a simple and sensitive SERS immunosensing platform for AMD contaminants in animal-derived foods. In this indirect competitive-type SERS-based immunoassay strategy, core-shell AuNRs@Ag served as the SERS active substrate could enhance the Raman signal of 4-MBA, significantly improving the detection sensitivity. The MBs served as the SERS capture probe to immobilize mAbs, could not only simplify the detection procedure, but also reduce the mAbs inactivation, improving the detection speed and stability. This proposed SERS immunosensing platform exhibited the merits of good accuracy, high sensitivity and specificity, acceptable stability in the quantitative analysis of AMD, which can be applied for rapid screening of AMD-contaminated foods.

Jingying Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Mingfei Pan: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Kaixin Liu: Data curation, Investigation. Xiaoqian Xie: Formal analysis, Supervision. Shan Wang: Software, Validation. Liping Hong: Writing – review & editing. Shuo Wang: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2017YFC1600402), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31972147), Tianjin Graduate Research Innovation Project (No. 2020YJSS127) and the Natural Science Foundation of the Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (No. 2021MS03077).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130783.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jingying Yang is currently a master's student majoring in Food Science, State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her research mainly focuses on the development of novel nano SERS probe and substrate and applied them in the analysis of food and environmental contaminants. She has published several academic papers in well-known journals such as Nanomaterials, Foods, Food Analytical Methods.

Mingfei Pan was serving as an associate professor in State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. His research mainly focuses on the development of novel biomimetic or biosensing devices based on various nanomaterials including their applications in food analysis and environment monitoring. He has published a few widely cited research papers in international core journals such as Biosensors and Bioelectronics, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, Foods etc.

Kaixin Liu is currently a master's student majoring in Food Science, State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her main research direction is developing new nanomaterials-based substrates for electrochemical biomimetic sensor and further applied them in food hazardous substances monitoring.

Xiaoqian Xie is currently a master's student majoring in Food Science, State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. She has been working on developing novel nanomaterials-based fluorescence sensors for food analysis.

Shan Wang is currently a master's student in State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her research mainly focuses on the development of nanomaterials-based chemical sensors and their application in food analysis.

Liping Hong is currently a master's student majoring in Food and Nutrition at Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her main research direction is to develop highly sensitive biomimetic sensing detection methods for various contaminants in foods.

Shuo Wang received his Ph.D. in form The University of Sydney, now he is serving as a specially appointed professor/doctoral supervisor, in State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. He has long been engaged in the research fields of Food Safety and Food Science and elected as the academic Fellow of International Academy of Food Science and Technology (IAFoST) in 2017.
